figures.caption: Fig. 1: Number of decision variables in the TSP as the number of vertices V increases. The red curve represents the number of variables in the standard formulation (n.var without CAF), while the green curve shows the number of variables after applying the CAF preprocessing procedure (n.var with CAF).

TABLE II: Comparison of the size of the TSP ILP formulation without and with the CAF preprocessing procedure. Each row corresponds to a different instance size defined by the number of vertices V . The table reports: Without CAF: number of decision variables in the complete ILP formulation, With CAF: number of decision variables after applying the CAF preprocessing procedure, GAP: percentage reduction in the number of variables obtained by CAF with respect to the complete formulation.

TABLE IV: Results for the TSP comparing performance with and without the CAF method. Each row corresponds to a different instance size defined by the number of vehicles V . The table reports: OF opt: optimal objective value obtained with Gurobi, OF Avg: average objective value across ten runs, OF Std: standard deviation of the objective value, GAP: average optimality gap with respect to OFopt, % Solved: percentage of instances solved within the time limit, Time Avg: average total time in seconds (including instance construction and solution time), Time Std: standard deviation of the total time, Time Solve Avg: average solution time in seconds, Time Solve Std: standard deviation of the solution time. The last row (AVG) reports the average values for each column across all tested instances.

TABLE III: Computational results for the complete ILP formulation on instances with an increasing number of vertices V , comparing the model performance without and with the CAF preprocessing procedure. Each row corresponds to a different instance size defined by the number of vertices V . The table reports: OF: objective function value returned by the solver, Time: total computational time in seconds required to run the model (including instance construction and solution time), Time Solve: time in seconds required by the solver to solve the optimization model, GAP Opt: percentage optimality gap with respect to the proven optimal solution returned by the solver.
